title: Artificial Intelligence
description: Comprehensive AI course covering ML, DL, and NLP

chapters.title: Machine Learning Basics
chapters.description: Introduction to Machine Learning concepts

blocks.content: What is Machine Learning?
Machine Learning is a branch of AI that enables computers to learn from data.
It uses algorithms to make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed.
Types of ML
Supervised Learning: Training with labeled data.
Unsupervised Learning: Finding patterns in unlabeled data.
Reinforcement Learning: Learning through rewards and punishments.
Applications of ML
Image recognition, NLP, fraud detection, recommendation systems, and more.
Data + Algorithms = Results
Challenges
Overfitting, underfitting, data quality, and computational complexity are common issues.
Feature selection and preprocessing are crucial for effective models.
Summary
ML allows systems to learn patterns from data and make intelligent predictions.
What are Neural Networks?
Neural Networks are computing systems inspired by the human brain.
They consist of layers of interconnected neurons that process data.
Perceptrons
The simplest type of neural network, representing a single neuron.
Perceptrons take input, apply a weight, and produce an output based on a threshold.
Multi-Layer Perceptrons
Combine multiple perceptrons into layers to solve complex problems.
Hidden layers enable the network to learn abstract representations of data.
Activation functions like ReLU and Sigmoid introduce non-linearity.
Deep Neural Networks
Networks with many hidden layers capable of handling complex tasks.
Used in image recognition, NLP, and reinforcement learning.
Training requires large datasets and significant computational power.
Summary
Neural Networks model complex patterns in data and form the basis of modern AI.

lessons.title: Deep Learning
lessons.description: Advanced ML techniques using multiple layers

blocks.content: Deep Learning Overview
Deep Learning is a subset of Machine Learning that uses neural networks with many layers.
It excels in tasks like image and speech recognition.
Convolutional Neural Networks
CNNs are designed for image data processing.
They use convolutional and pooling layers to extract features.
Recurrent Neural Networks
RNNs are specialized for sequential data like text or time series.
They maintain a memory of previous inputs to capture context.
Variants include LSTM and GRU networks.
Applications
Self-driving cars, voice assistants, image generation, and medical diagnostics.
Deep Learning requires GPUs and large datasets for training.
Summary
Deep Learning is a powerful tool for solving complex AI problems.

chapters.title: Natural Language Processing
chapters.description: Processing human language with AI

blocks.content: What is NLP?
NLP allows machines to understand and generate human language.
Applications include chatbots, translation, and sentiment analysis.
Text Preprocessing
Tokenization, stemming, and lemmatization prepare text for analysis.
Stop word removal and lowercasing help clean the text.
Word Embeddings
Word2Vec, GloVe, and FastText convert words into vectors.
Embeddings capture semantic meaning in a numeric form.
Embeddings are used as input to neural networks.
Applications
Machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots, text summarization.
NLP is used in search engines and recommendation systems.
Summary
NLP enables computers to interpret human language effectively.

title: Web Development
description: Full-stack web development covering HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and backend technologies

chapters.title: Frontend Fundamentals
chapters.description: Core technologies for building web interfaces

lessons.title: HTML Basics
lessons.description: Structure of web pages using HTML

blocks.content: What is HTML?
HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language and defines the structure of web pages.
It uses elements represented by tags to organize content.
HTML Document Structure
Every HTML document starts with a DOCTYPE declaration followed by html, head, and body tags.
The head contains metadata while the body holds visible content.
Hello World
Common HTML Tags
Headings (h1-h6), paragraphs (p), links (a), images (img), and lists (ul, ol) are the most used tags.
Semantic tags like header, nav, main, and footer improve readability and accessibility.
Forms and Inputs
Forms allow users to submit data using input, textarea, select, and button elements.
The action and method attributes control where and how data is sent.
Summary
HTML is the backbone of every web page, providing structure and meaning to content.
What is CSS?
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the visual appearance of HTML elements.
It can be applied inline, internally via a style tag, or externally via a linked file.
Selectors and Properties
Selectors target elements by tag, class, or ID. Properties like color, font-size, and margin define the style.

h1 { color: blue; font-size: 32px; }
.card { background: white; border-radius: 8px; }
Box Model
Every element is a box consisting of content, padding, border, and margin.
Understanding the box model is essential for precise layout control.
Flexbox and Grid
Flexbox is used for one-dimensional layouts, aligning items in rows or columns.
CSS Grid enables complex two-dimensional layouts with rows and columns.
Summary
CSS transforms plain HTML into visually appealing, responsive web interfaces.
What is JavaScript?
JavaScript is a scripting language that makes web pages interactive and dynamic.
It runs in the browser and can also run on the server via Node.js.
Variables and Data Types
JavaScript supports strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and objects.
Use let and const to declare variables; avoid var in modern code.
DOM Manipulation
The Document Object Model (DOM) represents the page as a tree of nodes.
JavaScript can select, modify, add, or remove DOM elements dynamically.
Events and Functions
Functions encapsulate reusable logic. Events like click, keydown, and submit trigger code execution.
Summary
JavaScript brings web pages to life with interactivity, animations, and real-time data.

chapters.title: Backend Development
chapters.description: Server-side programming and APIs

blocks.content: What is Node.js?
Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine for server-side development.
It is event-driven and non-blocking, making it efficient for I/O-heavy applications.
Express Framework
Express is a minimal web framework for Node.js that simplifies routing and middleware.
REST APIs
REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to manage resources.
Data is typically exchanged in JSON format between client and server.
Summary
Node.js and Express provide a fast, scalable foundation for backend web services.

chapters.title: Databases
chapters.description: Storing and querying data

blocks.content: What is SQL?
SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to manage and query relational databases.
Popular SQL databases include MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite.
CRUD Operations
Create, Read, Update, and Delete are the four fundamental database operations.

SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18;
INSERT INTO users (name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 25);
Joins and Relationships
JOIN operations combine rows from multiple tables based on a related column.
INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL joins cover different matching scenarios.
Summary
SQL is an essential skill for storing, retrieving, and managing structured data.

title: Data Science
description: Complete data science pipeline from collection and cleaning to analysis and visualization

chapters.title: Data Collection and Cleaning
chapters.description: Gathering and preparing raw data for analysis

lessons.title: Data Sources
lessons.description: Where and how to collect data

blocks.content: Types of Data Sources
Data can come from databases, APIs, web scraping, surveys, sensors, and flat files.
Structured data fits in tables; unstructured data includes text, images, and audio.
APIs and Web Scraping
REST APIs provide data in JSON or XML format via HTTP requests.
Web scraping uses tools like BeautifulSoup and Scrapy to extract data from HTML pages.
Data Formats
Common formats include CSV, JSON, XML, Parquet, and Excel spreadsheets.
Pandas is the primary Python library for reading and working with tabular data.
Summary
Knowing where to find and how to collect data is the first step in any data science project.
Why Clean Data?
Raw data is rarely perfect — it contains missing values, duplicates, and errors.
Poor data quality leads to inaccurate models and misleading insights.
Handling Missing Values
Missing values can be removed, filled with the mean/median, or predicted using models.

df.dropna()  # Remove rows with missing values
df['age'].fillna(df['age'].mean())  # Fill with mean
Outlier Detection
Outliers are extreme values that skew analysis. They can be detected using Z-scores or IQR.
Depending on context, outliers may be removed, capped, or kept.
Data Normalization
Normalization scales features to a common range, improving model performance.
Min-max scaling and Z-score standardization are the most common techniques.
Summary
Clean data is the foundation of reliable analysis and trustworthy machine learning models.

chapters.title: Data Analysis and Visualization
chapters.description: Exploring and presenting data insights

lessons.title: Exploratory Data Analysis
lessons.description: Understanding data through statistics and plots

blocks.content: What is EDA?
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the process of summarizing and visualizing data to find patterns.
It helps identify relationships, anomalies, and distributions before building models.
Descriptive Statistics
Mean, median, mode, standard deviation, and variance summarize the central tendency and spread.
Correlation measures the linear relationship between two variables.
Visualization Tools
Matplotlib and Seaborn are popular Python libraries for creating charts and plots.
Common plots include histograms, scatter plots, box plots, and heatmaps.
Summary
EDA gives a clear picture of the data before any formal modeling begins.

chapters.title: Statistical Modeling
chapters.description: Building predictive and inferential models

blocks.content: What is Regression?
Regression models predict a continuous target variable based on one or more input features.
Linear regression assumes a straight-line relationship between inputs and output.
Simple vs Multiple Regression
Simple regression uses one predictor; multiple regression uses two or more.

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
Evaluation Metrics
MSE, RMSE, and R-squared are used to assess regression model performance.
Lower error and higher R-squared indicate a better fit.
Summary
Regression is a fundamental technique for forecasting and understanding variable relationships.

title: Cybersecurity
description: Principles of securing systems, networks, and data from digital attacks

chapters.title: Network Security
chapters.description: Protecting network infrastructure and communications

blocks.content: OSI Model and TCP/IP
The OSI model has 7 layers from physical to application, each handling specific communication tasks.
TCP/IP is the practical protocol suite that powers the internet.
Common Protocols
HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, FTP, SSH, and SMTP are widely used protocols with distinct security profiles.
HTTPS encrypts communication using TLS, while plain HTTP transmits data in cleartext.
Firewalls and IDS
Firewalls filter traffic based on rules; Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) monitor for suspicious activity.
Next-generation firewalls (NGFW) include deep packet inspection and application awareness.
Summary
A solid understanding of networking is essential for identifying and defending against attacks.
Types of Cyber Attacks
Phishing, malware, man-in-the-middle, SQL injection, and DDoS are among the most frequent attacks.
Social engineering manipulates human psychology rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities.
Phishing and Social Engineering
Phishing emails trick users into revealing credentials or downloading malicious files.
Spear phishing targets specific individuals with personalized content.
Malware Types
Viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, and spyware each cause harm in different ways.
Ransomware encrypts victim files and demands payment for the decryption key.
Denial of Service
DoS and DDoS attacks flood servers with traffic, making services unavailable to legitimate users.
Botnets are used to distribute DDoS traffic across thousands of compromised machines.
Summary
Understanding attack vectors enables security teams to build effective defense strategies.

chapters.title: Cryptography
chapters.description: Mathematical foundations of data security

blocks.content: What is Encryption?
Encryption transforms readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using an algorithm and key.
Only parties with the correct key can decrypt and read the original data.
Symmetric Encryption
The same key is used for both encryption and decryption. AES is the most widely used symmetric cipher.
Fast and efficient, but the key must be securely shared between parties.
Asymmetric Encryption
Uses a public-private key pair. The public key encrypts; only the private key decrypts.
RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) are common asymmetric algorithms.
Hashing
Hash functions produce a fixed-size digest from any input. SHA-256 is widely used.
Hashes are one-way — they cannot be reversed, making them ideal for password storage.
Summary
Cryptography is the core mechanism for ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data.

chapters.title: Ethical Hacking
chapters.description: Authorized techniques to test system security

blocks.content: What is Penetration Testing?
Penetration testing (pen testing) is an authorized simulated attack to evaluate a system's security.
The goal is to find vulnerabilities before malicious actors do.
Phases of a Pen Test
Reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, and reporting are the five core phases.
Each phase builds on the previous one to achieve deeper system access.
Common Tools
Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, Wireshark, and John the Ripper are popular pen testing tools.
Kali Linux is a specialized OS bundling hundreds of security and testing tools.
Legal and Ethical Considerations
Pen testing must always be done with written permission. Unauthorized testing is illegal.
A scope agreement defines which systems can be tested and what methods are allowed.
Summary
Ethical hacking is a proactive approach to identifying and fixing security weaknesses.

title: Data Structures & Algorithms
description: A comprehensive deep-dive into Data Structures and Algorithms — from fundamentals to advanced techniques used in competitive programming and real-world systems.

chapters.title: Introduction to Algorithms
chapters.description: Foundations of algorithmic thinking, complexity analysis, and problem-solving strategies.

blocks.content: What is an Algorithm?

An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined instructions used to solve a problem or perform a computation.

Key properties of a good algorithm:
- Input: Takes zero or more inputs
- Output: Produces at least one output
- Definiteness: Each step is clearly defined
- Finiteness: Terminates after a finite number of steps
- Effectiveness: Every step is basic enough to be carried out
Example: Algorithm to find the maximum of two numbers
Why Algorithms Matter

Algorithms are the backbone of every software application. A poorly chosen algorithm can make a program thousands of times slower than necessary — even on powerful hardware.

Example: Sorting 1 million elements:
- Bubble Sort: ~1,000,000,000,000 operations
- Merge Sort: ~20,000,000 operations
Big-O Notation

Big-O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's runtime or space usage as input size (n) grows.

Common complexities (from best to worst):
- O(1)       — Constant
- O(log n)   — Logarithmic
- O(n)       — Linear
- O(n log n) — Linearithmic
- O(n²)      — Quadratic
- O(2ⁿ)      — Exponential
- O(n!)      — Factorial
O(1) — Constant Time Example
O(n) — Linear Time Example
O(n²) — Quadratic Time Example
Space Complexity

Space complexity measures how much memory an algorithm uses relative to input size.

Example:
- Storing a copy of an array → O(n) space
- Using a few variables → O(1) space

Always aim to balance time and space trade-offs.
Recursion

Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve a smaller subproblem.

Every recursive function must have:
1. A base case — the termination condition
2. A recursive case — reduces the problem size
Example: Factorial
Example: Fibonacci
The Call Stack

Each recursive call is pushed onto the call stack. Too many recursive calls without a base case leads to a Stack Overflow error.

Time complexity of naive Fibonacci: O(2ⁿ)
This is why memoization and dynamic programming are critical.

chapters.title: Arrays & Strings
chapters.description: The most fundamental data structures in computer science.

lessons.title: Arrays Deep Dive
lessons.description: Memory layout, operations, and complexity of arrays.

blocks.content: Arrays

An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations.

Access by index: O(1)
Search (unsorted): O(n)
Insert at end: O(1) amortized
Insert at beginning: O(n)
Delete at end: O(1)
Delete at beginning: O(n)
Array Declaration
Two-Pointer Technique

The two-pointer technique uses two indices that move toward each other to solve problems in O(n) instead of O(n²).

Example: Check if an array is a palindrome.
Sliding Window Technique

The sliding window technique efficiently processes subarrays/substrings of a fixed or variable size.

Example: Find max sum subarray of size k.

lessons.title: String Manipulation
lessons.description: String algorithms and common interview problems.

blocks.content: Strings

A string is a sequence of characters. In most languages, strings are immutable (cannot be changed after creation).

Common operations:
- Length: O(1) or O(n) depending on language
- Concatenation: O(n)
- Substring search: O(n*m) naive, O(n) with KMP
Reverse a String
Check Anagram
Two strings are anagrams if they contain the same characters with the same frequencies.
KMP Algorithm — Efficient String Search
The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm finds a pattern in a text in O(n + m) time by precomputing a failure function (LPS array) that avoids redundant comparisons.

chapters.title: Linked Lists
chapters.description: Singly, doubly, and circular linked lists with full implementation.

lessons.title: Singly Linked List
lessons.description: Structure and operations on singly linked lists.

blocks.content: Singly Linked List

A linked list is a linear data structure where elements (nodes) are linked via pointers.

Each node contains:
- data: the value
- next: pointer to the next node

Advantages over arrays:
- Dynamic size
- O(1) insertion/deletion at head

Disadvantages:
- No random access — O(n) to reach index
- Extra memory for pointers
Node & LinkedList Class
Detect a Cycle — Floyd's Algorithm
Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise and hare) uses two pointers moving at different speeds to detect cycles in O(n) time and O(1) space.
Reverse a Linked List

lessons.title: Doubly Linked List
lessons.description: Bidirectional traversal with doubly linked lists.

blocks.content: Doubly Linked List

A doubly linked list has nodes with two pointers:
- next: points to the next node
- prev: points to the previous node

This allows O(1) deletion when given a pointer to the node, and bidirectional traversal.

chapters.title: Stacks & Queues
chapters.description: LIFO and FIFO structures with real-world applications.

blocks.content: Stack

A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure.

Operations (all O(1)):
- push(x): Add element on top
- pop(): Remove top element
- peek(): View top element
- isEmpty(): Check if empty

Real-world uses:
- Undo/redo in editors
- Browser back button
- Function call stack
- Balanced parentheses checking
Stack Implementation
Balanced Parentheses

lessons.title: Queue
lessons.description: Queue implementation, circular queue, and priority queue.

blocks.content: Queue

A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure.

Operations (all O(1) with proper implementation):
- enqueue(x): Add to back
- dequeue(): Remove from front
- front(): View front element
- isEmpty(): Check if empty

Real-world uses:
- Task scheduling
- BFS traversal
- Printer job queue
- Message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka)
Queue using two Stacks
Priority Queue (Min-Heap based)

A priority queue dequeues elements in order of priority (not insertion order).

Implemented efficiently using a binary heap:
- Insert: O(log n)
- Extract min/max: O(log n)
- Peek min/max: O(1)

Used in: Dijkstra's algorithm, Huffman coding, A* pathfinding.

chapters.title: Trees
chapters.description: Binary trees, BSTs, AVL trees, and tree traversals.

lessons.title: Binary Trees
lessons.description: Structure and traversals of binary trees.

blocks.content: Binary Trees

A binary tree is a tree where each node has at most 2 children: left and right.

Terminology:
- Root: topmost node
- Leaf: node with no children
- Height: longest path from root to leaf
- Depth: distance from root to a node
- Full tree: every node has 0 or 2 children
- Complete tree: all levels filled except possibly last
- Perfect tree: all leaves at the same level
Tree Node Class
Tree Traversals

There are 4 main traversal orders:

1. Inorder (Left → Root → Right) — gives sorted output for BST
2. Preorder (Root → Left → Right) — useful for tree copy
3. Postorder (Left → Right → Root) — useful for tree deletion
4. Level-order (BFS) — level by level
Binary Search Tree

A BST is a binary tree where:
- Every node in the left subtree has value < current node
- Every node in the right subtree has value > current node

Average complexities:
- Search: O(log n)
- Insert: O(log n)
- Delete: O(log n)

Worst case (unbalanced): O(n)
BST Insert & Search
Validate a BST

lessons.title: AVL Trees & Balancing
lessons.description: Self-balancing BSTs for guaranteed O(log n) operations.

blocks.content: AVL Trees

An AVL tree (Adelson-Velsky and Landis) is a self-balancing BST.

Balance factor of a node = height(left) - height(right)

Valid balance factors: -1, 0, 1

If the balance factor becomes < -1 or > 1, rotations are performed to rebalance.

Rotation types:
1. Left Rotation (LL case)
2. Right Rotation (RR case)
3. Left-Right Rotation (LR case)
4. Right-Left Rotation (RL case)
Height and Balance Factor
Right Rotation

chapters.title: Heaps
chapters.description: Min-heaps, max-heaps, and heap sort.

blocks.content: Heap

A heap is a complete binary tree satisfying the heap property:

- Min-Heap: parent ≤ children (root is minimum)
- Max-Heap: parent ≥ children (root is maximum)

Heaps are typically stored in arrays:
- Parent of i: Math.floor((i - 1) / 2)
- Left child of i: 2*i + 1
- Right child of i: 2*i + 2
Min-Heap Implementation
Heap Sort

Heap sort builds a max-heap, then repeatedly extracts the max.

Time complexity: O(n log n)
Space complexity: O(1) — in-place

Not stable, but very memory efficient.

chapters.title: Hash Tables
chapters.description: Hashing, collision resolution, and applications.

blocks.content: Hash Table

A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function.

Average case:
- Insert: O(1)
- Search: O(1)
- Delete: O(1)

Worst case (all collisions): O(n)

A good hash function distributes keys uniformly to minimize collisions.
Simple Hash Function
Collision Resolution Strategies

1. Chaining (Separate Chaining):
   Each bucket holds a linked list of colliding entries.
   Easy to implement; performance degrades if chains grow long.

2. Open Addressing:
   - Linear Probing: Check next slot sequentially
   - Quadratic Probing: Check slots at quadratic intervals
   - Double Hashing: Use a second hash function for step size
Hash Map with Chaining
Classic Hash Map Problems

chapters.title: Graphs
chapters.description: Graph theory, representations, traversals, and classic algorithms.

lessons.title: Graph Fundamentals
lessons.description: Types, representations, and basic graph concepts.

blocks.content: Graphs

A graph G = (V, E) consists of:
- V: a set of vertices (nodes)
- E: a set of edges (connections)

Types of graphs:
- Undirected: edges have no direction
- Directed (Digraph): edges have direction
- Weighted: edges have weights/costs
- Cyclic / Acyclic
- Connected / Disconnected
- DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph
Graph Representations

1. Adjacency Matrix:
   - 2D array of size V×V
   - matrix[i][j] = 1 if edge exists
   - Space: O(V²)
   - Edge lookup: O(1)

2. Adjacency List:
   - Array of lists
   - Space: O(V + E)
   - Edge lookup: O(degree)
   - Preferred for sparse graphs
Breadth-First Search (BFS)

BFS explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper.

Uses a queue.
Time: O(V + E)
Space: O(V)

Applications:
- Shortest path in unweighted graph
- Level-order traversal
- Finding connected components
Depth-First Search (DFS)

DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking.

Uses a stack (or recursion).
Time: O(V + E)
Space: O(V)

Applications:
- Topological sort
- Cycle detection
- Pathfinding
- Finding SCCs
Dijkstra's Algorithm

Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative weights.

Time: O((V + E) log V) with a priority queue
Space: O(V)

Steps:
1. Set distance to source = 0, all others = Infinity
2. Use a min-priority queue
3. For each extracted minimum, relax all neighbors
4. Repeat until queue is empty

lessons.title: Topological Sort
lessons.description: Ordering of vertices in a DAG.

blocks.content: Topological Sort

Topological sort produces a linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that for every directed edge u → v, u appears before v.

Applications:
- Task scheduling (build systems)
- Course prerequisites
- Dependency resolution (npm, gradle)

Two approaches:
1. Kahn's Algorithm (BFS-based)
2. DFS-based (post-order)
Kahn's Algorithm

chapters.title: Sorting Algorithms
chapters.description: Classic and advanced sorting algorithms with full analysis.

blocks.content: Bubble Sort

Bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent elements that are in the wrong order.

Time: O(n²) worst/average, O(n) best (already sorted)
Space: O(1)
Stable: Yes
Insertion Sort

Insertion sort builds a sorted array one element at a time.

Time: O(n²) worst/average, O(n) best
Space: O(1)
Stable: Yes

Excellent for small arrays and nearly sorted data.

lessons.title: Efficient Sorts
lessons.description: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Counting Sort.

blocks.content: Merge Sort

Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits the array in half, sorts each half, then merges them.

Time: O(n log n) always
Space: O(n)
Stable: Yes

Preferred for linked lists and external sorting.
Quick Sort

Quick sort picks a pivot and partitions the array around it.

Time: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst
Space: O(log n)
Stable: No

Fastest in practice due to excellent cache performance.
Counting Sort

Counting sort is a non-comparison sort for integers in a known range.

Time: O(n + k) where k = range
Space: O(k)
Stable: Yes

Extremely fast when k is small relative to n.

chapters.title: Searching Algorithms
chapters.description: Linear search, binary search, and advanced search strategies.

lessons.title: Binary Search
lessons.description: Divide and conquer search on sorted arrays.

blocks.content: Binary Search

Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space.

Time: O(log n)
Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive

Requirement: array must be sorted.
Binary Search Variants

chapters.title: Dynamic Programming
chapters.description: Memoization, tabulation, and classic DP problems.

lessons.title: Introduction to DP
lessons.description: What is dynamic programming and when to use it.

blocks.content: Dynamic Programming

Dynamic Programming (DP) solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation.

Two conditions for DP:
1. Overlapping subproblems
2. Optimal substructure

Two approaches:
- Top-Down (Memoization): Recursive + cache
- Bottom-Up (Tabulation): Iterative + table
Fibonacci — Three Ways

lessons.title: Classic DP Problems
lessons.description: 0/1 Knapsack, Longest Common Subsequence, Coin Change.

blocks.content: 0/1 Knapsack Problem

Given items with weights and values, and a knapsack of capacity W, maximize the total value without exceeding capacity.

Time: O(n * W)
Space: O(n * W)
Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)

Find the longest subsequence common to two strings.

Time: O(m * n)
Space: O(m * n)

Example: LCS('ABCBDAB', 'BDCAB') = 'BCAB' (length 4)
Coin Change

Given coin denominations and a target amount, find the minimum number of coins needed.

Time: O(amount * coins.length)
Space: O(amount)

lessons.title: Advanced DP
lessons.description: Matrix Chain Multiplication, Edit Distance, DP on Trees.

blocks.content: Edit Distance (Levenshtein Distance)

Find the minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert string A to string B.

Time: O(m * n)
Space: O(m * n)

Used in: spell checkers, diff tools, DNA alignment.
Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)

Find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

- DP approach: O(n²)
- Binary search approach: O(n log n)

chapters.title: Greedy Algorithms
chapters.description: Making locally optimal choices for globally optimal solutions.

lessons.title: Greedy Strategy
lessons.description: When greedy works and classic greedy problems.

blocks.content: Greedy Algorithms

A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find the global optimum.

Greedy works when the problem has:
1. Greedy choice property: local optimal → global optimal
2. Optimal substructure

Greedy does NOT always give optimal solutions (e.g., 0/1 knapsack). DP is needed when greedy fails.
Activity Selection Problem

Select the maximum number of non-overlapping activities.

Greedy strategy: Always pick the activity that finishes earliest.
Huffman Coding

Huffman coding assigns shorter codes to more frequent characters.

Used in: ZIP compression, JPEG, MP3.

Algorithm:
1. Build a min-heap of character frequencies
2. Repeatedly merge two lowest-frequency nodes
3. Assign 0 to left branches, 1 to right branches

Time: O(n log n)
Fractional Knapsack

Unlike 0/1 knapsack, you can take fractions of items.

Greedy: Sort by value/weight ratio, take as much as possible.

chapters.title: Advanced Data Structures
chapters.description: Tries, Segment Trees, Union-Find, and more.

lessons.title: Trie (Prefix Tree)
lessons.description: Efficient string storage and prefix search.

blocks.content: Trie

A Trie (from re-TRIE-val) is a tree where each node represents a character.

Operations:
- Insert: O(m) where m = word length
- Search: O(m)
- StartsWith: O(m)

Applications:
- Autocomplete / search suggestions
- Spell checking
- IP routing tables

lessons.title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
lessons.description: Efficiently manage disjoint sets with union and find.

blocks.content: Union-Find

Union-Find (DSU) tracks elements grouped into disjoint sets.

Operations:
- find(x): Find the root/representative of x's set — O(α(n)) amortized
- union(x, y): Merge sets of x and y — O(α(n)) amortized

α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function — practically constant.

Applications:
- Kruskal's MST algorithm
- Cycle detection in graphs
- Connected components

lessons.title: Segment Tree
lessons.description: Range queries and point updates in O(log n).

blocks.content: Segment Tree

A segment tree is a binary tree used for range queries (sum, min, max) and point updates.

Build: O(n)
Query: O(log n)
Update: O(log n)
Space: O(n)

Alternative: Fenwick Tree (BIT) — simpler, lower constant factor, but less general.
